stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) be the linear transformation with standard matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) (so \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \) in the standard basis). Let \( B = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2\} \) be the basis of \( \mathbb{R}^2 \) with \( \mathbf{b}_1 = ([[p]],\ [[q]]) \) and \( \mathbf{b}_2 = ([[r]],\ [[s]]) \). If \( [T]_B \) is the matrix of \( T \) relative to \( B \) (used for both the domain and codomain), what is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( [T]_B \) (the coefficient of \( \mathbf{b}_1 \) when \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \) is written in the basis \( B \))?
solution: Compute \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) = ([[u1]],\ [[u2]]) \) in standard coordinates. Writing \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) = x\mathbf{b}_1 + y\mathbf{b}_2 \) gives the system \( [[p]]x+[[r]]y=[[u1]] \), \( [[q]]x+[[s]]y=[[u2]] \); solving for \( x \) gives the \((1,1)\) entry \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the standard-coordinate first entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), not its coefficient of \( \mathbf{b}_1 \) in the basis \( B \).
This is the standard-coordinate second entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), mistaken for the \((1,1)\) entry.
This is the negative of the correct entry, from a sign error solving the \(2\times2\) system.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This mistakes a coordinate of the basis vector \( \mathbf{b}_1 \) itself for the matrix entry.
This mistakes an entry of the standard matrix \(A\) for the entry of \([T]_B\).

stem: Let \( B = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2\} \) be the basis of \( \mathbb{R}^2 \) with \( \mathbf{b}_1 = ([[p]],\ [[q]]) \) and \( \mathbf{b}_2 = ([[r]],\ [[s]]) \). The change-of-basis matrix from the standard basis to \( B \) is the matrix of the identity transformation relative to the standard basis (domain) and \( B \) (codomain). Using it to write \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]],\ [[v2]]) \) as \( \mathbf{v} = x\mathbf{b}_1 + y\mathbf{b}_2 \), what is \( x \) (the \((1,1)\) entry of the change-of-basis matrix applied to \( \mathbf{v} \))?
solution: Solving \( [[p]]x+[[r]]y=[[v1]] \), \( [[q]]x+[[s]]y=[[v2]] \) for \( x \) (unique since \( \mathbf{b}_1,\mathbf{b}_2 \) are independent) gives \( x = [[answer]] \), the \( B \)-coordinate of \( \mathbf{v} \) along \( \mathbf{b}_1 \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the standard first coordinate of \( \mathbf{v} \) itself for its \(B\)-coordinate.
This mistakes the standard second coordinate of \( \mathbf{v} \) itself for its \(B\)-coordinate.
This is the negative of the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This mistakes a coordinate of \( \mathbf{b}_1 \) for the answer.
This mistakes a coordinate of \( \mathbf{b}_2 \) for the answer.

stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3 \) have standard matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[k]] \end{bmatrix} \). Let \( B = \{\mathbf{b}_1,\mathbf{b}_2,\mathbf{b}_3\} \) be the basis of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) with \( \mathbf{b}_1=([[p1]],[[p2]],[[p3]]) \), \( \mathbf{b}_2=([[q1]],[[q2]],[[q3]]) \), \( \mathbf{b}_3=([[r1]],[[r2]],[[r3]]) \). If \( [T]_B \) is the matrix of \( T \) relative to \( B \) (for both domain and codomain), what is the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( [T]_B \) (the coefficient of \( \mathbf{b}_1 \) when \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \) is written in the basis \( B \))?
solution: Compute \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) = ([[u1]],\ [[u2]],\ [[u3]]) \) in standard coordinates, then solve \( x\mathbf{b}_1+y\mathbf{b}_2+z\mathbf{b}_3 = T(\mathbf{b}_1) \) for \( x \), giving the \((1,1)\) entry \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is a standard-coordinate entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), not its coefficient of \( \mathbf{b}_1 \) in \( B \).
This is a standard-coordinate entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), mistaken for the \((1,1)\) entry.
This is a standard-coordinate entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), mistaken for the \((1,1)\) entry.
This is the negative of the correct entry, from a sign error solving the \(3\times3\) system.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.

stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) have standard matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). Let \( B = \{\mathbf{b}_1,\mathbf{b}_2\} \) with \( \mathbf{b}_1=([[b1x]],[[b1y]]) \), \( \mathbf{b}_2=([[b2x]],[[b2y]]) \) be a basis for the DOMAIN, and let \( C = \{\mathbf{c}_1,\mathbf{c}_2\} \) with \( \mathbf{c}_1=([[c1x]],[[c1y]]) \), \( \mathbf{c}_2=([[c2x]],[[c2y]]) \) be a (different) basis for the CODOMAIN. If \( [T]_{C \leftarrow B} \) denotes the matrix of \( T \) relative to \( B \) (domain) and \( C \) (codomain), what is its \( (1,1) \) entry (the coefficient of \( \mathbf{c}_1 \) when \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \) is written in the basis \( C \))?
solution: Compute \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) = ([[u1]],\ [[u2]]) \) in standard coordinates, then write it in the codomain basis \( C \): \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) = x\mathbf{c}_1+y\mathbf{c}_2 \). Solving \( [[c1x]]x+[[c2x]]y=[[u1]] \), \( [[c1y]]x+[[c2y]]y=[[u2]] \) for \( x \) gives the \((1,1)\) entry \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the standard-coordinate first entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), not its coefficient of \( \mathbf{c}_1 \) in \( C \).
This is the standard-coordinate second entry of \( T(\mathbf{b}_1) \), mistaken for the \((1,1)\) entry.
This is the negative of the correct entry, from a sign error solving the \(2\times2\) system.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This mistakes a coordinate of \( \mathbf{c}_1 \) itself for the matrix entry.
This mistakes a coordinate of \( \mathbf{c}_1 \) itself for the matrix entry.
